meta.title: F16 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X6 F16 engine — N20, N55, N63, S63 and N57. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the N55 vs N57 vs V8 choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X6 F16 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F16 engine typically costs £2,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N20 four-cylinder petrol sits at £2,000–£3,800, while the S63 V8 in the X6 M can reach £8,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,560. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F16 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55B30 3.0-litre straight-six (xDrive35i) — BMW's more robust single-turbo replacement for the N54 — delivers 306 hp with solid reliability and significantly fewer HPFP and injector issues than its predecessor. The N57 3.0-litre diesel (xDrive30d) is also robust and torque-rich, with many owners reporting 200,000+ miles with proper servicing. Both significantly outlast the N20 four-cylinder and N63/S63 V8s in terms of trouble-free mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X6 F16?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N20 four-cylinder (xDrive28i), oil consumption and turbo failure on the N63 V8 (xDrive50i) due to hot-vee heat management issues, electric water pump failure on the N55 straight-six, DPF and EGR clogging on the N57 diesel range (xDrive30d, xDrive40d, M50d), and — unrelated to the engine — air suspension compressor failure on all F16s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X6 F16 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and maintenance history. The N55 straight-six and N57 diesels are the most reliable — they have proven durability and are well-supported by the BMW specialist community. The N20 four-cylinder carries a timing chain risk that requires careful inspection. The N63 V8 is characterful but suffers from heat-related issues; the S63 in the X6 M is a masterpiece but demands deep pockets. A well-maintained F16 with the right engine can be a reliable luxury SUV.

mainEntity.name: Which F16 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the N20 xDrive28i without documented timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N63 V8 xDrive50i without documented service history is also a watch-item due to hot-vee heat management issues. The S63 X6 M is a fantastic engine but maintenance costs are significant — budget accordingly. For most buyers, the N55 xDrive35i or N57 xDrive30d represents the safest choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F16 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: cold-start rattle (N20 timing chain), blue smoke on start-up or excessive oil consumption (N63 V8 valve stem seals/turbos), overheating or coolant loss (N55 water pump), DPF warning lights or loss of power (N57 diesel), or knocking or loss of power (S63 issues). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F16 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £18,000 X6, a £2,500–£4,800 N57 recon is 15–30% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £7,000 example, the same recon is 35–70% — borderline. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do F16 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 straight-six routinely exceeds 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The N57 diesel is similarly durable — many owners report 200,000+ miles. The N20 can also reach high mileages but the timing chain is the limiting factor — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The N63 and S63 V8s can also reach high mileages but maintenance costs are significantly higher.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E71, F16 and G06?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E71 (2008–2014) is the first-generation X6 — the original 'Sports Activity Coupé' with a four-seat layout, based on the E70 X5 platform. The F16 (2014–2019, this page) is the second-generation X6 — more refined, with five seats, updated engines including the N55 and N57, and based on the F15 X5 platform. The G06 (2019–present) is the third-generation X6 — with the latest B-series engines (B58, B47), a more modern interior, and a more aggressive design. Costs and reliability profiles differ — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW F16 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on four-cylinder engines (N20) it's on the left-hand side; on six-cylinder engines (N55, N57) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover; on V8s (N63, S63) it's on the front of the block. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW X6 F16 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second‑generation X6 — from the N20 four‑cylinder petrol and N55 straight‑six to the N63 and S63 V8s, plus the N57 diesel family including the triple‑turbo M50d. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's Sports Activity Coupé. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X6 F16 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second‑generation X6 (F16) specifically — from the December 2014 launch to the July 2019 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any F16 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (AustralianCar.Reviews, BMW forums, reliability guides). The N55B30 is widely regarded as a reliable single‑turbo straight‑six — an evolution of the N54 that addressed HPFP and injector issues. The N57 diesel family is generally robust, though DPF and EGR issues are common; the N57S (M50d) triple‑turbo adds complexity. The N20 four‑cylinder carries the highest risk due to timing chain stretch (shared with F30 N20 issues). The N63 V8 suffers from hot‑vee heat management problems; the S63 in the X6 M is a masterpiece but demands deep pockets.

overview.intro: The F16 is the second‑generation BMW X6, launched at the 2014 Paris Motor Show and produced from December 2014 to July 2019. It succeeded the E71 and brought a more refined, luxurious interior, updated technology, and a wider range of engines. Built on the same L4 platform as the F15 X5, the F16 retained the sloping "coupé SUV" silhouette that defined the original X6, but offered five proper seats instead of the first‑generation's four. Under the bonnet, the F16 offered a comprehensive range of petrol and diesel engines: from the N20 four‑cylinder and N55 straight‑six petrols to the N63 and S63 V8s, plus the N57 diesel family — including the triple‑turbo M50d.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L4 (shared with F15 X5) • Predecessor: BMW X6 (E71) • Successor: BMW X6 (G06) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (F16 is 5‑door SAC only; F86 is X6 M).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F16 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on xDrive30d diesel and xDrive35i petrol variants as these luxury SUVs enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The N55B30 3.0‑litre straight‑six (xDrive35i) — BMW's more robust single‑turbo replacement for the N54 — delivers 306 hp with solid reliability and significantly fewer HPFP and injector issues than its predecessor. The N57 3.0‑litre diesel (xDrive30d) is also robust and torque‑rich, with many owners reporting 200,000+ miles with proper servicing. The N20 four‑cylinder carries the highest risk — timing chain stretch is a well‑documented issue shared with F30 N20 engines. The N63 V8 (xDrive50i) suffers from hot‑vee heat management problems and oil consumption. The S63 (X6 M) is a masterpiece but demands deep pockets for maintenance. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained N55 or N57 with documented service history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risky Four — avoid without chain history
Hot‑Vee V8 — heat management is the weakness
Most Expensive — supercar costs in an SUV

economicsRule.title: The F16 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,000 N57 recon against a £6,000–£25,000 X6 sits at 10–80% of the car's value. On a sub‑£8,000 F16, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £18,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 15–30% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N20 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N20's timing chain guides are made of plastic that degrades over time — leading to chain stretch, rattling on start‑up, and eventual catastrophic engine failure if the chain snaps.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle on an xDrive28i, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N20 engine prices for your F16 →

problems.title: N63 V8 Oil Consumption & Turbo Failure
problems.rootCause: The N63's hot‑vee design leads to heat‑related issues — valve stem seals degrade causing oil consumption, and turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation.
problems.recommendation: If your V8‑powered F16 produces blue smoke on start‑up or shows excessive oil consumption, get the valve stem seals and turbos inspected — £800–£1,800 now is cheaper than the £4,500–£8,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your F16 →

problems.title: N55 Electric Water Pump Failure
problems.rootCause: The N55's electric water pump is a known weak point — failure leads to overheating, loss of cooling, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any N55‑powered F16, budget for water pump replacement at 80k miles — a £300–£600 preventative job is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,800 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare N55 engine prices for your F16 →

problems.title: N57 Diesel DPF & EGR Clogging
problems.affectedModels: F16 xDrive30d, xDrive40d, M50d (2014–2019) with N57 engine.
problems.rootCause: The N57 diesel's DPF and EGR system clog with soot, particularly on urban‑use vehicles — leading to reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and eventual engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If your diesel‑powered F16 is used primarily for short trips, a £150–£400 DPF and EGR service every 20k miles is cheap insurance against the £600–£1,200 replacement that follows.

cta.label: Compare N57 diesel DPF repair quotes →

problems.title: F16 Air Suspension Compressor Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All F16 variants with rear air suspension, particularly high‑mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on the F16 — the rear air suspension compressor fails or the system develops leaks, causing the vehicle to sit unevenly or fail to raise.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension inspected on any F16 over 60,000 miles — it's one of the most common reasons an otherwise sound X6 becomes a costly ownership proposition.

cta.label: Compare F16 air suspension repair quotes →
